provenanceJourney.event: Carved for the Parthenon under Phidias
Purchased from Elgin by Act of Parliament for £35,000
Acropolis Museum opens with space reserved for their return

redFlags.evidence: Removed from the Parthenon by agents of Lord Elgin between 1801 and 1812 under a disputed Ottoman authorisation. The original firman does not survive; what its terms permitted has been argued over ever since.
Greece — a UNESCO 1970 source country with a formally lodged and continuously pressed restitution request.

repatriation.note: Greece has sought return since the 19th century. The Acropolis Museum opened in 2009 with the space reserved. UNESCO's intergovernmental committee has repeatedly urged a bilateral settlement; the British Museum cites the British Museum Act 1963 as barring deaccession.

sources.title: UNESCO — return and restitution of cultural property

registryChecks.registry: INTERPOL Stolen Works of Art Database
registryChecks.method: Domain-scoped search of interpol.int. This searches the registry's public WEBSITE, not its register.
registryChecks.caveat: No evidence surfaced through the access available. This is NOT a statement that the object is absent from this register, and it is NOT a clean bill of health — objects looted at source were never inventoried and cannot appear in a stolen-property register at all.

registryChecks.method: No programmatic interface exists. Deep link emitted for a human to run the authoritative search.
registryChecks.caveat: This register could not be searched programmatically. Nothing about the object has been established either way — the official search below must be run by a human.
